title: Ear oiling (karna purana) explained
excerpt: Karna purana, the old habit of putting warm oil in the ears, is meant to soothe vata, soften dryness and ease stubborn earwax. Here is the gentle, safe way to do it at home.
body_html:
why ears go dry and itchy in the first place

You know that small dry itch deep in the ear that you cannot quite reach, the faint ringing on a windy day, or the way cotton buds seem to make things worse, not better. Many of us reach for a bud, dig around, and end up with a sorer, drier ear than before. Ayurveda looks at all of this through one familiar lens: vata, the dry, light, moving quality in the body that tends to rise as we age, in cold weather, after travel, and with too much screen-led, restless living.

The classical texts treat the ear as a special seat of vata. The senses each have a home, and sound and the ear belong closely to the air principle (Sushruta Samhita, Sharirasthana). When vata grows dry and rough here, the symptoms are very ordinary: itching, a blocked feeling, mild ringing, stiffness in the jaw and neck, hardened wax. The Ayurvedic answer to dryness is not more poking but its opposite, snigdha, the oily and soft. That is the whole idea behind karna purana, which simply means filling the ear with oil.

The Ayurvedic answer to dryness is not more poking but its opposite, snigdha, the oily and soft.

what karna purana is and how the texts use it

Karna purana is named among the daily and seasonal self-care routines in the classical canon. Ashtanga Hridaya places it within dinacharya, the everyday regimen meant to keep vata settled and the sense organs nourished (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 2). The reasoning is plain: warm oil held briefly in the ear lubricates the canal, softens accumulated wax so it can move out on its own, and quiets the dry, vata symptoms of itch, blockage and ringing.

Sushruta describes ear care and ear treatments in his work on the head and senses, where oil is the gentle, nourishing measure for the ear and its surroundings (Sushruta Samhita, Uttaratantra). The texts favour plain warm sesame oil, often lightly medicated, because sesame (tila taila) is the classic vata-pacifying oil: warming, unctuous and stable. The point is never to flood or force the ear. It is a small, calm, warming ritual, the oily quality answering the dry one.

a safe way to try it at home

If your ears are healthy, this is a gentle thing to do once or twice a week, or at the change of seasons when dryness rises. Warm a little plain sesame oil to body temperature, never hot. Lie on your side, let two or three drops fall into the upper ear, and rest like that for a few minutes while you gently pull the outer ear and work the jaw. Then turn over and let the oil drain onto a tissue. Wipe only the outer ear; do not push anything inside.

Keep it simple and unhurried. Body-warm oil, a few drops, a few quiet minutes, and let gravity do the draining. A warm cloth held against the outer ear afterward feels pleasant and helps any softened wax loosen. Done this calmly, karna purana is one of those small evening habits that leaves the ears feeling less dry and the head a little quieter.

when to skip it and see a doctor

Do not put oil in the ear if you have any pain, discharge, recent ear surgery, a known or suspected hole in the eardrum (perforation), or if you ever get a runny ear after a cold. In those situations oil can sit behind a problem and make it worse. Sudden hearing loss, severe pain, fever with ear pain, blood or fluid from the ear, dizziness, or ringing that does not settle are all red flags: see a doctor or a qualified vaidya promptly rather than treating at home.

This is general information drawn from the classical texts, not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical care. Ears are delicate. If anything feels wrong, or if symptoms are severe, persistent or worrying, get them looked at by a professional before trying any home practice.

Try This Today

  • Warm the oil to body temperature: Stand the bottle of plain sesame oil in a cup of warm water for a minute, or warm a spoonful gently. Test a drop on your wrist: it should feel comfortably warm, never hot.
  • Lie on your side and add a few drops: Rest on one side, let two or three drops fall into the upper ear, and stay there for three to five minutes while you gently tug the outer ear and move your jaw to help the oil settle.
  • Let gravity drain it: Turn over onto the other side, let the oil run out onto a tissue, and wipe only the outer ear. Do not push a bud or anything else into the canal.
  • Keep it occasional: Once or twice a week, or at the change of seasons when dryness rises, is plenty for healthy ears. More is not better.

metafields.dek: Karna purana, the old habit of putting warm oil in the ears, is meant to soothe vata, soften dryness and ease stubborn earwax. Here is the gentle, safe way to do it at home.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Karna purana means filling the ear with a little warm oil to calm dryness, itch and stubborn wax, all signs of vata.
The classical routine uses body-warm sesame oil, a few drops, a few quiet minutes on your side, then drain; never hot oil and never poking inside.
Skip it entirely with ear pain, discharge, a possible eardrum perforation or recent surgery, and see a doctor for any red-flag symptoms.

glossary_terms.definition: Literally filling the ear; the practice of putting warm oil into the ear.
The dry, light, moving quality in the body, closely linked to the ear and to sound.
The oily, soft, unctuous quality, used to counter dryness.
The daily self-care routine the texts recommend for keeping the body and senses well.
Sesame oil, the classic warming, vata-pacifying oil.

sources: Sushruta Samhita, Uttaratantra (ear diseases and ear care, Shalakya section)
Sushruta Samhita, Sharirasthana (the ear as a functional seat of vata)

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Karna purana" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Karna purana is defined as: Literally filling the ear; the practice of putting warm oil into the ear.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to warm the oil to body temperature. Stand the bottle of plain sesame oil in a cup of warm water for a minute, or warm a spoonful gently. Test a drop on your wrist: it should feel comfortably warm, never hot.

faqs.question: Are there any specific lifestyle habits I should adopt?
faqs.answer: A key suggestion is to lie on your side and add a few drops. Rest on one side, let two or three drops fall into the upper ear, and stay there for three to five minutes while you gently tug the outer ear and move your jaw to help the oil settle.
